meta.originalTitle: The History of Tea 茶叶的历史

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.

The story of tea begins in China. According to legend, in 2737 BC the Chinese emperor Shen Nung was sitting beneath a tree while his servant boiled drinking water, when some leaves from the tree blew into the water. Shen Nung, a renowned herbalist, decided to try the infusion that his servant had accidentally created. The tree was a Camellia sinensis, and the resulting drink was what we now call tea. It is impossible to know whether there is any truth in this story, but tea-drinking certainly became established in China many centuries before it had even been heard of in the West. Containers for tea have been found in tombs dating from the Han Dynasty (206 BC–220 AD), but it was under the Tang Dynasty (618–906 AD) that tea became firmly established as the national drink of China.

It became such a favourite that during the late eighth century a writer called Lu Yu wrote the first book entirely about tea, the Ch’a Ching, or Tea Classic. It was shortly after this that tea was first introduced to Japan, by Japanese Buddhist monks who had travelled to China to study. Tea received almost instant imperial sponsorship and spread rapidly from the royal court and monasteries to the other sections of Japanese society.

So at this stage in the history of tea, Europe was rather lagging behind. In the latter half of the sixteenth century there are the first brief mentions of tea as a drink among Europeans, mostly from Portuguese who were living in the East as traders and missionaries. But although some of these individuals may have brought back samples of tea to their native country, it was not the Portuguese who were the first to ship back tea as a commercial import.

This was done by the Dutch, who in the last years of the sixteenth century began to encroach on Portuguese trading routes in the East. By the turn of the century they had established a trading post on the island of Java, and it was via Java that in 1606 the first consignment of tea was shipped from China to Holland. Tea soon became a fashionable drink among the Dutch, and from there spread to other countries in continental western Europe, but because of its high price it remained a drink for the wealthy.

Britain, always a little suspicious of continental trends, had yet to become the nation of tea-drinkers that it is today. Starting in 1600, the British East India Company had a monopoly on importing goods from outside Europe, and it is likely that sailors on these ships brought tea home as gifts. The first coffee-house had been established in London in 1652, and tea was still somewhat unfamiliar to most readers, so it is fair to assume that the drink was still something of a curiosity.

Gradually, it became a popular drink in coffee-houses, which were as much locations for the transaction of business as they were for relaxation or pleasure. They were, though, the preserve of middle- and upper-class men; women drank tea in their own homes, and as yet tea was still too expensive to be widespread among the working classes. In part, its high price was due to a punitive system of taxation.

One unforeseen consequence of the taxation of tea was the growth of methods to avoid taxation—smuggling and adulteration. By the eighteenth century many Britons wanted to drink tea but could not afford the high prices, and their enthusiasm for the drink was matched by the enthusiasm of criminal gangs to smuggle it in. What began as a small-time illegal trade, selling a few pounds of tea to personal contacts, developed by the late eighteenth century into an astonishing organised-crime network, perhaps importing as much as seven million lb annually, compared to a legal import of five million lb!

Worse for the drinkers was that taxation also encouraged the adulteration of tea, particularly of smuggled tea which was not quality-controlled through customs and excise. Leaves from other plants, or leaves which had already been brewed and then dried, were added to tea leaves. By 1784, the government realised that enough was enough, and that heavy taxation was creating more problems than it was worth. The new Prime Minister, William Pitt the Younger, slashed the tax from 119 per cent to 12.5 per cent. Suddenly legal tea was affordable, and smuggling stopped virtually overnight.

Another great impetus to tea-drinking resulted from the end of the East India Company's monopoly on trade with China, in 1834. Before that date, China was the country of origin of the vast majority of the tea imported to Britain, but the end of its monopoly stimulated the East India Company to consider growing tea outside China. India had always been the centre of the Company's operations, which led to the increased cultivation of tea in India, beginning in Assam. There were a few false starts, including the destruction by cattle of one of the earliest tea nurseries, but by 1888 British tea imports from India were for the first time greater than those from China.

The end of the East India Company's monopoly on trade with China also had another result, which was more dramatic though less important in the long term: it ushered in the era of the tea clippers. While the Company had had the monopoly on trade, there was no rush to bring the tea from China to Britain, but after 1834 the tea trade became a virtual free-for-all.

Individual merchants and sea-captains with their own ships raced to bring home the tea and make the most money, using fast new clippers which had sleek lines, tall masts and huge sails. In particular, there was competition between British and American merchants, leading to the famous clipper races of the 1860s. But these races soon came to an end with the opening of the Suez Canal, which made the trade routes to China viable for steamships for the first time.
from the passage for each answer.
Researchers believe the tea containers detected in
from the Han Dynasty were the first evidence of the use of tea.
about tea before anyone else in the eighth century.
from Japan who brought tea to their native country from China.
Tea was carried from China to Europe by the
The British government had to cut the taxation on tea because of the serious crime of
In order to compete in shipping speed, traders used

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：研究者认为，在汉朝_\___\__中发现的茶具，是茶叶使用的最早证据。
答案：tombs（古墓）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “Containers for tea have been found in tombs dating from the Han Dynasty (206 BC–220 AD)”，“detected in” 对应 “found in”，明确提到茶具发现于汉朝古墓，因此答案为 “tombs”。
题目翻译：8 世纪时，陆羽比任何人都早撰写了一本关于茶的_\___\__。
答案：book（书籍）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “a writer called Lu Yu wrote the first book entirely about tea”，“before anyone else” 对应 “the first”，明确提到陆羽撰写了首部关于茶的书籍，因此答案为 “book”。
题目翻译：是来自日本的_\___\__将茶从中国带回本国。
答案：monks（僧侣）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “it was first introduced to Japan, by Japanese Buddhist monks who had travelled to China to study”，明确提到日本佛教僧侣将茶引入日本，因此答案为 “monks”。
题目翻译：茶叶由_\___\__从中国运往欧洲。
答案：Dutch（荷兰人）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “This was done by the Dutch... in 1606 the first consignment of tea was shipped from China to Holland”，“carried” 对应 “shipped”，明确提到荷兰人首次将茶从中国运往欧洲，因此答案为 “Dutch”。
题目翻译：因严重的_\___\__犯罪，英国政府不得不降低茶叶税。
答案：smuggling（走私）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “One unforeseen consequence of the taxation of tea was the growth of methods to avoid taxation-smuggling and adulteration... the government realised that enough was enough”，明确提到走私是政府降税的重要原因，因此答案为 “smuggling”。
题目翻译：19 世纪时，除中国外，\___\___也开始种植茶叶。
答案：India（印度）
解析：定位 Paragraph 9 中 “the end of its monopoly stimulated the East India Company to consider growing tea outside China. India had always been the centre of the Company’s operations, which led to the increased cultivation of tea in India”，明确提到 19 世纪印度开始种植茶叶，因此答案为 “India”。
题目翻译：为在运输速度上竞争，商人使用_\___\__进行竞速。
答案：clippers（快船）
解析：定位 Paragraph 10 中 “Individual merchants and sea-captains with their own ships raced to bring home the tea... using fast new clippers”，明确提到商人使用 “快船（clippers）” 竞速运茶，因此答案为 “clippers”。

items.text:
（1）题目 1：Researchers believe the tea containers detected in \___\___ from the Han Dynasty were the first evidence of the use of tea.
题目翻译：研究者认为，在汉朝_\___\__中发现的茶具，是茶叶使用的最早证据。
答案：tombs（古墓）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “Containers for tea have been found in tombs dating from the Han Dynasty (206 BC–220 AD)”，“detected in” 对应 “found in”，明确提到茶具发现于汉朝古墓，因此答案为 “tombs”。

（2）题目 2：Lu Yu wrote a \___\___ about tea before anyone else in the eighth century.
题目翻译：8 世纪时，陆羽比任何人都早撰写了一本关于茶的_\___\__。
答案：book（书籍）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “a writer called Lu Yu wrote the first book entirely about tea”，“before anyone else” 对应 “the first”，明确提到陆羽撰写了首部关于茶的书籍，因此答案为 “book”。

（3）题目 3：It was \___\___ from Japan who brought tea to their native country from China.
题目翻译：是来自日本的_\___\__将茶从中国带回本国。
答案：monks（僧侣）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “it was first introduced to Japan, by Japanese Buddhist monks who had travelled to China to study”，明确提到日本佛教僧侣将茶引入日本，因此答案为 “monks”。

（4）题目 4：Tea was carried from China to Europe by the \___\___.
题目翻译：茶叶由_\___\__从中国运往欧洲。
答案：Dutch（荷兰人）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “This was done by the Dutch... in 1606 the first consignment of tea was shipped from China to Holland”，“carried” 对应 “shipped”，明确提到荷兰人首次将茶从中国运往欧洲，因此答案为 “Dutch”。

（5）题目 5：The British government had to cut the taxation on tea because of the serious crime of \___\___.
题目翻译：因严重的_\___\__犯罪，英国政府不得不降低茶叶税。
答案：smuggling（走私）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “One unforeseen consequence of the taxation of tea was the growth of methods to avoid taxation-smuggling and adulteration... the government realised that enough was enough”，明确提到走私是政府降税的重要原因，因此答案为 “smuggling”。

（6）题目 6：Tea was planted in \___\___ besides China in the 19th century.
题目翻译：19 世纪时，除中国外，\___\___也开始种植茶叶。
答案：India（印度）
解析：定位 Paragraph 9 中 “the end of its monopoly stimulated the East India Company to consider growing tea outside China. India had always been the centre of the Company’s operations, which led to the increased cultivation of tea in India”，明确提到 19 世纪印度开始种植茶叶，因此答案为 “India”。

（7）题目 7：In order to compete in shipping speed, traders used \___\___ for the race.
题目翻译：为在运输速度上竞争，商人使用_\___\__进行竞速。
答案：clippers（快船）
解析：定位 Paragraph 10 中 “Individual merchants and sea-captains with their own ships raced to bring home the tea... using fast new clippers”，明确提到商人使用 “快船（clippers）” 竞速运茶，因此答案为 “clippers”。

children.text: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 8–13 on your answer sheet, write
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
Tea was popular in Britain in the 16th century.
Tea was more fashionable than coffee in Europe in the late 16th century.
Tea was enjoyed by all classes in Britain in the 17th century.
The adulteration of tea also prompted William Pitt the Younger to reduce the tax.
Initial problems occurred when tea was planted outside China by the East India Company.
The fastest vessels were owned by America during the 19th-century clipper races.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：16 世纪时，茶在英国很流行。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “in the latter half of the sixteenth century there are the first brief mentions of tea as a drink among Europeans... it was not the Portuguese who were the first to ship back tea as a commercial import”，16 世纪英国仅简要提及茶，尚未商业化进口，更谈不上 “流行”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：16 世纪末，茶在欧洲比咖啡更时尚。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：文章仅提到 16 世纪末茶在欧洲被提及，17 世纪伦敦开设首家咖啡馆，未比较 “茶与咖啡的时尚程度”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：17 世纪时，英国所有阶层都饮用茶。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “they were the preserve of middle- and upper-class men; women drank tea in their own homes, and as yet tea was still too expensive to be widespread among the working classes”，明确提到 17 世纪茶仅限中上层及女性在家饮用，工薪阶层因高价无法享用，并非 “所有阶层”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：茶叶掺假也促使小威廉・皮特降低茶税。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “One unforeseen consequence of the taxation of tea was the growth of methods to avoid taxation-smuggling and adulteration... the government realised that enough was enough... William Pitt the Younger, slashed the tax”，明确提到 “走私和掺假” 共同促使政府降税，掺假是原因之一，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：东印度公司在中国以外地区种植茶叶时，初期遇到了问题。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 9 中 “There were a few false starts, including the destruction by cattle of one of the earliest tea nurseries”，“false starts” 对应 “initial problems”，明确提到初期种植遇挫（如茶园遭牲畜破坏），与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：19 世纪快船竞速期间，最快的船只为美国所有。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 11 仅提到 “英美商人竞争激烈”，未提及 “美国拥有最快船只”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

items.text:
（1）题目 8：Tea was popular in Britain in the 16th century.
题目翻译：16 世纪时，茶在英国很流行。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “in the latter half of the sixteenth century there are the first brief mentions of tea as a drink among Europeans... it was not the Portuguese who were the first to ship back tea as a commercial import”，16 世纪英国仅简要提及茶，尚未商业化进口，更谈不上 “流行”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（2）题目 9：Tea was more fashionable than coffee in Europe in the late 16th century.
题目翻译：16 世纪末，茶在欧洲比咖啡更时尚。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：文章仅提到 16 世纪末茶在欧洲被提及，17 世纪伦敦开设首家咖啡馆，未比较 “茶与咖啡的时尚程度”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（3）题目 10：Tea was enjoyed by all classes in Britain in the 17th century.
题目翻译：17 世纪时，英国所有阶层都饮用茶。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “they were the preserve of middle- and upper-class men; women drank tea in their own homes, and as yet tea was still too expensive to be widespread among the working classes”，明确提到 17 世纪茶仅限中上层及女性在家饮用，工薪阶层因高价无法享用，并非 “所有阶层”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（4）题目 11：The adulteration of tea also prompted William Pitt the Younger to reduce the tax.
题目翻译：茶叶掺假也促使小威廉・皮特降低茶税。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “One unforeseen consequence of the taxation of tea was the growth of methods to avoid taxation-smuggling and adulteration... the government realised that enough was enough... William Pitt the Younger, slashed the tax”，明确提到 “走私和掺假” 共同促使政府降税，掺假是原因之一，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（5）题目 12：Initial problems occurred when tea was planted outside China by the East India Company.
题目翻译：东印度公司在中国以外地区种植茶叶时，初期遇到了问题。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 9 中 “There were a few false starts, including the destruction by cattle of one of the earliest tea nurseries”，“false starts” 对应 “initial problems”，明确提到初期种植遇挫（如茶园遭牲畜破坏），与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（6）题目 13：The fastest vessels were owned by America during the 19th-century clipper races.
题目翻译：19 世纪快船竞速期间，最快的船只为美国所有。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 11 仅提到 “英美商人竞争激烈”，未提及 “美国拥有最快船只”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
